title: 【ハンガリー総選挙 オルバン体制に終止符？】米ロから支持も“EUの異端児”は劣勢／元側近・マジャル党首が不満の受け皿に／EUの“地殻変動”につながるか／争点となるウクライナ支援

transcript.text: There is an election in Hungary on April
the 12th. Victor Orban is behind by
double-digit percentage points.
>> For more than a decade and a half,
Victor Orban has outlasted almost
everyone in European politics.
>> He has proven very adept at holding on
to power and he clearly has a great
appetite for power. But he and the
hungry he shaped are under pressure from
stagnant growth, high inflation,
mounting scandals, and now a formidable
opponent.
>> Hungary's election is not just important
for voters in Hungary, but also in
Europe, the US, as well as in Russia.
Whoever is in power will have a very
hard time over the coming years to
address both societal, economic, and
political concerns.
>> And what happens in Hungary won't stay
in Hungary. Inside Europe, it wields
influence far beyond its size.
>> The loan remains blocked because one
leader is not honoring his word.
>> So, how did Orban create one of Europe's
most durable political machines? Why is
it now under strain? And no matter who
wins in April, why does Hungary's next
move affect the rest of the world?
As a member of the European Union, even
small countries can veto decisions of
global significance, and Hungary is one
of them.
Hungary's allies in both the EU and NATO
have become used to Orban, opposing many
of their joint initiatives.
>> Hungary plans to block every EU decision
concerning Ukraine until oil starts
flowing again through the uh Drisbel
in Ukraine. Hungary has dragged its feet
at NATO's expansion when it took in
eventually Finland as well as Sweden,
but only after serious foot dragging
from Orban. Very often it seems that the
interest that the Hungarian government
is trying to protect is that of Russia
and sometimes maybe even China,
>> including via doubling down on Russian
energy contracts at a time when the EU
is trying to tighten sanctions.
>> Much of that stance reflects the
priorities of one man, Prime Minister
Victor Orban, and his desire to move
past the country's former Soviet
occupation. He has cultivated ties in
Moscow, in Washington, and Beijing.
>> He also waded into US politics and
endorsed Donald Trump during his first
presidential campaign,
>> and Trump returned a favor recently,
endorsing Orban,
>> but that relationship is rooted in a
system the prime minister began building
years earlier. Orban first won office in
1998 as the leader of the right-wing
Fedes party. He lost office in 2002, but
not forever.
>> Orban returned to power after 8 years in
opposition in 2010.
>> He came back to power with a mission to
institute regime change
>> and to build what he later called an
illiberal state, an ideology that stems
from his disdain of liberal democracy.
Almost the very first thing he did was
to pass a law changing the electoral
system.
>> In effect, critics say, helping entrench
Fedz's advantage. And that was only the
start. Orban also pushed through a new
constitution.
>> This included removing effectively the
top Supreme Court justice.
>> He also managed to install his political
loyalists in data institutions as well
as the media. Newspapers were taken over
by his business allies.
>> Some they actually just shut down.
>> That allowed him to essentially create a
national echo chamber.
>> Public media functions as a megaphone
communication channel for the governing
party.
>> It has that kind of sense of constantly
cultivating images of an enemy of a
threat from outside. For years, that
grip helped him control the political
story, even as economic strains mounted.
After returning to power on a wave of
anger over the financial crisis, Orban
presided over years of modest growth,
rising wages, and new factory jobs. His
government delivered tangible financial
benefits to families. It's just that
with a sputtering economy, these have
become increasingly costly to sustain.
Economic growth and inflation have been
really the twin problems of the Orban
government. Hungary had the highest
levels of inflation during after the
pandemic and Hungarians had been really
struggling to make ends meet over the
past several years.
Adding to the pressure, since 2015,
house prices in Hungary have more than
tripled, outpacing the EU average and
rising faster than incomes for many
Hungarians.
It's a far cry from the tailwinds Orban
enjoyed upon his return to office
following the initial challenge of
overcoming the effects of the global
financial crisis. He was very lucky at
the start. The world entered a period of
low inflation and global growth and then
loads of European Union money flooded in
and critics say that actually a lot of
that luck was wasted because it was
plowed into kind of visible projects
like you know pritification of town
squares, prestige building and things
like that. Meanwhile, neighbors like
Poland pulled ahead as Hungary lost
momentum.
>> Whether it's income per capita or
household spending or cancer survival
rates or number of universities in the
global top thousand and it's sort of now
kind of at the back of the queue whereas
once it was at the top.
>> Hungary was doing relatively well after
its accession to the EU. But if we look
at the trajectory, we see that the
Hungarian economy is lagging behind
whereas Poland is taking off. The
government has had to retrench
investments, including in public
services that were never really well
funded to begin with. Orban himself has
had to pass an executive order to stock
hospitals with toilet paper.
Now, big industrial bets were meant to
restart growth and fix a lot of that,
but so far they've fallen short.
>> Orban has sought to position Hungary as
a bridge between east and west,
including economically,
where, for example, Hungary serves as a
hub for German car companies, especially
during their transition to the electric
vehicle era. So he's brought in a lot of
Asian, Chinese, South Korean battery
makers in order to boost Hungary's
economy.
>> He hasn't really delivered because the
transition to electric vehicles has gone
slightly more slowly than we expected
and because the German economy on which
Hungary relies for exports is itself
struggling. Then there were a series of
scandals coming out of the battery
factories relating to leaks of toxic
chemicals into the environment. At the
same time, anger grew over the widening
gap between a well-connected elite and
ordinary Hungarians.
>> Corruption has been one of the biggest
problems under the Orban administration.
According to Transparency International,
Hungary has plunged in rankings all the
way to last place in the European Union.
Taken together, a stagnant economy,
failing services, and deep corruption
created a powder keg. That opening was
seized by Peter Maja, a former insider
of Orban's ruling elite.
>> It took an insider turned critique to
credibly delivered the message that the
system is rotten.
>> Using the once marginal teaser party as
his vehicle, Madar largely sidestepped
Orban's media machine.
>> He's visited almost every city, every
hamlet. He's walked hundreds of
kilometers to meet voters. The new
opposition party managed to not only win
over the supporters of old opposition
parties from the left and the liberals
in the capital, but also build a strong
base all over the country.
>> His appeal is simple. Keep what voters
like, strip out the corruption, fix
public services, and repair Hungary's
relationship with the EU. Madam thread a
very small needle appealing to
disillusioned Federas voters without
alienating the center. If you thought
Fedas was the right kind of thing then
you probably are going to think his
party tit is kind of okay as well. He is
the main receptacle for just about
anybody who wants to get rid of Orban.
>> So faced with a real challenger Orban
returned to a familiar playbook defining
a new enemy. The campaign strategy of
Fidas has been built on fear-mongering.
It was before the center left and uh the
liberal opposition, Brussels and EU
institutions, George Shoros, migrants,
LGBTQ communities, and now it's the war.
>> Orban has made Ukraine essentially the
centerpiece of his campaign. There are
some really eye-catching posters up
around the country. The message is that
life in Hungary is tough, sure, but it
could be so much worse. And these cheeky
Ukrainians are prepared to risk all of
that.
>> Orban is also portraying himself as a
strong leader who is taken seriously by
global leaders, but it less and less
resonates across the society.
Even if Orban Fides loses this election,
it doesn't mean that Fidas would lose
power in the system.
>> He will still be the top of a party with
immense power, lots of money, control of
media assets, and a demonstrated will to
power. Essentially,
>> there are captured state institutions
which could continue to represent
Fides's interests and could
significantly block the actions of a new
government.
>> Orban is the last politician that
anybody would count out.
